architecture.summary: The system uses sparse autoencoders (SAEs) applied to internal activations of Llama-3.1-8B and Gemma-2-9B to identify and classify feature populations by their functional role along two independent dimensions: correctness and confidence. A 2×2 partitioning framework categorizes model predictions, SAEs extract features for each quadrant independently, and targeted suppression of identified features measures their causal contribution to accuracy and entropy. Features are categorized as pure uncertainty, pure incorrectness, or confounded; suppression experiments and selective abstention via learned classifiers form the central mechanism.

biblio.title: Are LLM Uncertainty and Correctness Encoded by the Same Features? A Functional Dissociation via Sparse Autoencoders

claims.statement: Pure uncertainty features are functionally essential: suppressing them severely degrades accuracy

Pure incorrectness features are functionally inert: despite showing statistically significant activation differences between correct and incorrect predictions, the majority produce near-zero change in accuracy when suppressed
Targeted suppression of confounded features yields a 1.1% accuracy improvement and a 75% entropy reduction, with effects transferring across the ARC-Challenge and RACE benchmarks
The activations of just 3 confounded features from a single mid-network layer predict model correctness (AUROC ~0.79)
Selective abstention using the 3-feature correctness predictor raises accuracy from 62% to 81% at 53% coverage
Uncertainty and correctness are distinct internal phenomena, encoded by separate feature populations rather than the same mechanisms
